message:
br-bd-ntb4l lock down crtime end-to-end through FsOps surface

statx(STATX_BTIME) on Linux can't reach FUSE userspace today — the
vendored `fuser` ships only the Linux-flavoured `fuse_attr`, which
has no crtime field, so `to_file_attr` drops it at the protocol
boundary. The kernel-FUSE protocol's `FUSE_STATX` opcode (proto 7.35,
libfuse 3.x) is the wire-level fix, and it requires upgrading the
vendored fuser; tracked as a follow-up.

What we *can* guarantee right now is that the in-process FsOps API —
the surface that any future statx wiring will read from — preserves
the on-disk birth time on every code path. ext4 already populates
`InodeAttr::crtime` from `Ext4Inode::crtime_system_time()` and ext4
`setattr` only touches atime/mtime/ctime (never crtime), but no test
locked the contract. Three new regressions:

- `ext4_create_returns_attrs_with_nonzero_crtime`: `create` returns
  an attr whose crtime is non-zero and matches ctime to second
  resolution at the create instant.
- `ext4_getattr_round_trips_crtime_from_disk`: `getattr` on the new
  inode surfaces the same crtime that `create` returned, proving the
  field survives the read path and the on-disk encode/decode.
- `ext4_setattr_mtime_does_not_modify_crtime`: `setattr` with a fresh
  mtime updates mtime but leaves crtime untouched, matching POSIX +
  ext4 semantics.

Tests: 636 ffs-core --lib pass (was 632 + 3 new + 1 from peer). fmt +
clippy clean. STATX_DIOALIGN / STATX_MNT_ID are kernel-side
concerns (queue limits + mount table), not FUSE-driver concerns.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

+{"id":"bd-4uyu5","title":"[FUSE] RWF_DSYNC + io_uring per-op flags propagation","description":"Honor pwritev2(2) RWF_* flags (RWF_SYNC, RWF_DSYNC, RWF_HIPRI, RWF_NOWAIT, RWF_APPEND) and io_uring per-SQE flags (IOSQE_FIXED_FILE, IOSQE_IO_LINK, IOSQE_ASYNC) when the kernel forwards them through the FUSE WRITE / IORING_OP_WRITE path.

Context:
- Linux pwritev2 with RWF_DSYNC must commit the write durably (data + minimal metadata) before returning. ext4 implements this via filemap_write_and_wait_range + ext4_sync_file. FrankenFS should map RWF_DSYNC to: stage write -> jbd2 commit_transaction -> dev.sync (data only, not full inode metadata journal).
- RWF_NOWAIT must return EAGAIN rather than block on a contended lock (matches ext4's mapping_check_writeback flag); for ffs-fuse, this means try_lock on FuseInodeLocks instead of blocking acquire.
- io_uring forwards fuse_in_header.flags including FUSE_IOCB_NOWAIT (bit 9) and FUSE_IOCB_DSYNC; the dispatcher needs to read these and route accordingly.

Acceptance:
- A test that issues pwritev2 with RWF_DSYNC, then drops the underlying device dirty cache, then re-mounts -> data is recoverable (no FUA but still durable per the journal commit).
- pwritev2 with RWF_NOWAIT on a parent inode currently held by another mutation returns EAGAIN within 100us, not a multi-ms block.
- xfstests generic/{623,633,634} (RWF_*) pass.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T05:25:56.562180780Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T05:25:56.562180780Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-ntb4l","title":"[FUSE] statx field handling: STATX_BTIME, STATX_DIOALIGN, STATX_MNT_ID","description":"Map statx(2) request masks to ext4/btrfs metadata fields end-to-end through ffs-fuse.

Context:
- Linux statx(STATX_BTIME) returns the inode birth time (crtime in ext4). FrankenFS already stores crtime/crtime_extra in Ext4Inode but the FUSE layer surfaces only the legacy stat fields. statx callers (rust std::fs::Metadata::created, e2fsprogs, find -newerB...) get 0 / EOPNOTSUPP.
- STATX_DIOALIGN (kernel 6.1+) reports memory + offset alignment for direct I/O. For ffs-fuse the alignment is the device block size, mirrored from BlockDevice::block_size().
- STATX_MNT_ID identifies the mount and lets userspace correlate cross-mount inode numbers; FUSE exposes it via the request kernel.
- Per ext4 spec §3.2 (ext4_inode), btime lives at i_crtime + i_crtime_extra. Per btrfs btrfs_inode_item.otime.

Acceptance:
- statx(fd, '', AT_EMPTY_PATH, STATX_BTIME, &out) returns the same crtime that's in the on-disk inode for files created through the mount.
- A new ffs-fuse test that opens a file via mounted FS, calls statx via libc, and asserts btime == ctime at create time and != ctime after touch -m.
- STATX_DIOALIGN populated with stx_dio_mem_align = stx_dio_offset_align = block_size for ext4 mounted with O_DIRECT support.","status":"in_progress","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T05:24:49.800611252Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T05:27:16.560041180Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

 {"id":"bd-p30s","title":"ext4_write_compressed fallback to uncompressed write loses sparsity","description":"In ext4_write_compressed, if the data is handed to write_ext4_uncompressed_cluster as a fallback, the input cluster_data is fully zero-padded to cluster_bytes. The uncompressed writer checks 'if start >= data.len() { /* create hole */ }' to skip trailing blocks, but since data.len() is always exactly cluster_bytes, this condition never evaluates to true. As a result, the uncompressed fallback unconditionally allocates and writes physical zero blocks for the entire tail of the cluster instead of maintaining holes/sparsity.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-18T18:23:15.087017986Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-18T19:25:04.732864086Z","closed_at":"2026-04-18T19:25:04.729949129Z","close_reason":"Preserve sparse tail when e2compr falls back to uncompressed cluster writes; added regression coverage and passed fmt/check/clippy + focused tests.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-p3sd","title":"FUSE E2E: fuse_spec_i5_delete_reclaims_free_counts fails at file 169","description":"The test creates 1000 files in a subdirectory then tries to delete them all. File 169 consistently returns ENOENT during fs::remove_file. File 169 is the first entry in the second directory block (the directory transitions from 1 block to 2 blocks at this point).

Root cause analysis:
- Core-level test (create 1000 + lookup + unlink) passes - the filesystem logic is correct
- The failure is FUSE-specific - kernel dentry cache or FUSE protocol interaction
- The MVCC store correctly holds all committed data
- Adding sync/flush between create and delete phases does not fix it
- The failure is 100% deterministic at file 169

Possible causes:
1. FUSE kernel negative dentry caching interfering with create/unlink sequence
2. Directory extent resolution issue specific to the FUSE lookup path under kernel cache pressure
3. Kernel dentry eviction + daemon lookup returning stale data

Next steps: Add FUSE-level tracing/logging to the lookup and unlink paths to capture the exact sequence of FUSE operations around file 169","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-03-27T19:06:40.043910040Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-27T19:37:10.917554076Z","closed_at":"2026-03-27T19:37:10.917280702Z","close_reason":"Fixed: checksum tail was not initialised in new directory blocks, causing parse_dir_block to fail with rec_len overflow","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
+{"id":"bd-p7vga","title":"CONFORMANCE: btrfs on-disk format golden round-trip harness","description":"# Family A (shipped 2026-04-24, closed in place)

Freezes the btrfs on-disk byte layouts owned by `ffs-btrfs` via 23 golden-fixture tests in `crates/ffs-btrfs/tests/conformance_ondisk_golden.rs`:

- INODE_ITEM (160 bytes fixed): regfile, dir-with-zero-times, u64 bounds, short-payload rejection.
- DIR_ITEM / DIR_INDEX: single entry, two-entry concatenation, 1-byte-name edge.
- INODE_REF: single-entry, hard-link concatenation, 255-byte NAME_MAX.
- EXTENT_DATA: regular (53 bytes), inline (21+data), short-header rejection, short-after-header rejection, unknown-type rejection.
- XATTR_ITEM: single name/value, empty-value edge.

 {"id":"bd-qpt5","title":"Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation","description":"# Add SIMD-accelerated checksum computation

## GOAL
Accelerate CRC32C and BLAKE3 checksum computation using SIMD instructions.

## BACKGROUND (Extreme Software Optimization)
Checksum computation is on the hot path:
- Every block read/write verifies checksum
- ARC cache hits still verify (trust but verify)
- RaptorQ symbols have checksums

SIMD can provide 4-16x speedup depending on instruction set.

## TECHNICAL REQUIREMENTS

### 1. CRC32C Acceleration
```rust
pub fn crc32c_simd(data: &[u8], seed: u32) -> u32 {
    #[cfg(target_arch = "x86_64")]
    {
        if is_x86_feature_detected!("sse4.2") {
            return crc32c_sse42(data, seed);
        }
    }
    #[cfg(target_arch = "aarch64")]
    {
        if is_aarch64_feature_detected!("crc") {
            return crc32c_arm_crc(data, seed);
        }
    }
    crc32c_fallback(data, seed)
}
```

### 2. BLAKE3 (Already SIMD)
- blake3 crate already uses SIMD
- Ensure proper feature flags enabled
- Benchmark to confirm

### 3. Batch Checksum
```rust
/// Compute checksums for multiple blocks in parallel
pub fn batch_checksum(blocks: &[&[u8]], algo: ChecksumAlgo) -> Vec<u32> {
    // Use rayon for multi-block parallelism
    // Use SIMD within each block
}
```

### 4. Feature Detection at Runtime
- Detect CPU features once at startup
- Store in global (or thread-local)
- Dispatch to appropriate implementation

## TESTS
1. Unit: SIMD matches fallback results
2. Unit: All supported CPUs tested (via CI matrix)
3. Benchmark: Before/after comparison
4. Property: Random data, SIMD == fallback

## LOGGING
- Feature detection (info): detected SIMD capabilities
- Batch checksum (trace): block count, duration

## ACCEPTANCE CRITERIA
1. Correctness: SIMD == fallback for all inputs
2. Performance: >2x speedup on supported CPUs
3. Graceful fallback on unsupported CPUs
4. No unsafe code (use safe SIMD wrappers)

## NOTES
Use existing crates:
- crc32c crate has SIMD support
- blake3 crate is already optimized
Main work is ensuring proper feature flags and benchmarking.","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-13T02:55:14.096946312Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163955333Z","closed_at":"2026-02-17T15:05:05.163936237Z","close_reason":"Completed","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["optimization","perf","simd"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:57:00.533583752Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","depends_on_id":"bd-3ib.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-13T03:53:29.930946133Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":207,"issue_id":"bd-qpt5","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"Alien uplift contract (auto-applied via extreme-software-optimization + alien-artifact-coding + alien-graveyard).

 {"id":"bd-qrqdk","title":"[REVIEW][MEDIUM] Jbd2Writer emits unchecksummed descriptor/revoke/commit blocks — replay with verify_checksums=true rejects them on checksummed journals","description":"# Review finding (review-only pass on 2026-04-23)

## Location

`crates/ffs-journal/src/lib.rs` `Jbd2Writer::commit_transaction` (lines ~982-1180) and the `Jbd2Writer` struct (~841-851).

## Finding

The JBD2-compatible writer produces three kinds of blocks — descriptor, revoke, and commit — and does not stamp a checksum into any of them:

- Descriptor blocks (L1056-L1095): built with `vec![0; bs]` + header + tags, written verbatim. No tail-checksum at `block[bs-4..bs]`.
- Revoke blocks (L1126-L1153): built with header + entries, written verbatim. No tail-checksum.
- Commit blocks (L1160-L1164): built with just `encode_jbd2_header`; `h_chksum_type`, `h_chksum_size`, and `h_chksum[0]` at offset 16 are all zero bytes. No CRC written.

The replay-side `verify_jbd2_block_checksum` (L282-L321) checks all three types when `sb.has_checksum()` is true:

- Descriptor/revoke: reads `stored = read_be_u32(block, bs-4)`, computes via `checksum_jbd2_tail_zeroed_block(block, seed)`, compares.
- Commit: reads `stored = read_be_u32(block, JBD2_COMMIT_CHKSUM_OFFSET)` (offset 16), computes `!crc32c_append(!seed, &temp)` with the 4 bytes at 16 zeroed, compares.

For writer-produced blocks, `stored == 0` in every case but `computed` is a non-zero CRC of the block bytes, so the comparison returns false and `replay_jbd2_inner` breaks out of the scan (L523).

## Impact

A writer-produced transaction on a checksummed journal (any of `JBD2_FEATURE_COMPAT_CHECKSUM`, `JBD2_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_CSUM_V2`, `JBD2_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_CSUM_V3`, or `JBD2_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_ASYNC_COMMIT`) is unreplayable with the default `ReplayOptions { verify_checksums: true }`. Modern `mkfs.ext4` enables `metadata_csum` by default, which sets `JBD2_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_CSUM_V3` on the journal superblock, so any real-world ext4 image with default features falls into this path.

## Why this is currently latent (but still a bug)

`Jbd2Writer::attach_jbd2_writer` is not called from any production binary today (grep across `crates/ffs-fuse`, `crates/ffs-cli`, `crates/ffs-harness`, `crates/ffs` finds no callers); only tests construct writers. Tests avoid triggering the bug by either running without a JBD2 superblock in the journal, or by post-stamping checksums via the `stamp_commit_checksum` / `stamp_descriptor_or_revoke_checksum` helpers (lines 2591-2608), which exist precisely because the writer does not.

When anyone wires `attach_jbd2_writer` into the `ffs mount --rw` / `commit_transaction_journaled` path (see `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs:4312`), a checksummed ext4 image will fail to replay on the next mount, silently losing the transaction's writes.

## Suggested fix

1. Add `has_checksum: bool` and `csum_seed: u32` fields to `Jbd2Writer` alongside `is_64bit`.
2. `Jbd2Writer::open` already reads the journal superblock (L889) — have it also populate these fields via `sb.has_checksum()` and `sb.csum_seed()`.
3. Provide a `Jbd2Writer::with_checksum_config(region, start_seq, has_checksum, csum_seed)` constructor for the `Jbd2Writer::new` callers that need it.
4. In `commit_transaction`, when `has_checksum` is true:
   - After filling a descriptor block but before `dev.write_block`, stamp the tail via `checksum_jbd2_tail_zeroed_block(&desc, self.csum_seed).unwrap()` and write into the last 4 bytes.
   - Same for revoke blocks.
   - For commit blocks, zero `h_chksum[0]` (offset 16), compute `!crc32c::crc32c_append(!seed, &commit)`, write into offset 16.
5. Fix `max_tags_per_descriptor` / `max_revoke_entries` / `blocks_needed` to reserve 4 tail bytes when `has_checksum` is true.

## Acceptance

- New regression test: build a `MemBlockDevice`, stamp a valid JBD2 superblock with `feature_incompat = JBD2_FEATURE_INCOMPAT_CSUM_V3` + a non-zero UUID at region block 0, call `Jbd2Writer::open` (or `with_checksum_config`), commit one transaction with a single write, then call `replay_jbd2_with_options(cx, dev, region, ReplayOptions { verify_checksums: true })` and assert `outcome.stats.replayed_blocks == 1` and the target block was overwritten with the payload.
- Existing non-checksummed tests keep passing.
- `commit_transaction_journaled` in `crates/ffs-core/src/lib.rs` — still has no production caller, but when it does, the journal round-trips through a verify_checksums=true replay.

## Severity

[MEDIUM] — real data-loss bug with a clear trigger (metadata_csum ext4 + Jbd2Writer write path), but unreachable from any shipping binary today. Worth fixing before anyone wires the writer into the mount --rw path.","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"bug","created_at":"2026-04-23T23:52:19.015585Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T00:58:44.436959096Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T00:58:44.435067924Z","close_reason":"Jbd2Writer stamps descriptor, revoke, and commit checksums for verified replay","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-z01zm","title":"[FUSE] overlayfs lower/upper redirect parity for whiteouts + redirect xattr","description":"Bring ffs-fuse to overlayfs(5)-as-upper-or-lower parity: handle whiteout char devices (0,0), opaque-dir markers (trusted.overlay.opaque), redirect xattrs (trusted.overlay.redirect), and metacopy markers (trusted.overlay.metacopy).

Context:
- overlayfs uses character device with rdev=makedev(0,0) for whiteouts. lookup must surface a hidden negative dentry; readdir must skip the whiteout entry.
- trusted.overlay.opaque='y' on a directory means: ignore lower-layer entries inside it. ffs-fuse must propagate this xattr through ffs-xattr (already supports trusted.* namespace) AND consult it in readdir to filter.
- trusted.overlay.redirect='/abs/path' marks a moved directory. The lookup must follow the redirect when descending.
- trusted.overlay.metacopy is a marker that the file's data is in the lower layer; reads still come from the upper inode for metadata, but data must be redirected. For a non-overlayfs FrankenFS mount this just needs to round-trip the xattr correctly.

Acceptance:
- Mount FrankenFS as the upper of an overlayfs mount; create + delete files; the deletion produces a 0,0 char device on the upper layer that the kernel respects.
- Mount FrankenFS as the lower; opaque-dir + redirect xattrs round-trip via ffs-xattr without loss.
- xfstests overlay/{006,037,069} pass with FrankenFS as upper layer.","status":"open","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-25T05:26:21.395361679Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-25T05:26:21.395361679Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

+{"id":"bd-z0rn9","title":"CONFORMANCE: MVCC WAL record format golden round-trip harness","description":"# Family C (shipped 2026-04-24, closed in place)

Freezes the MVCC WAL header + commit record layouts owned by `ffs-mvcc::wal` via 20 golden-fixture tests in `crates/ffs-mvcc/tests/conformance_wal_golden.rs`:

## Header (16 bytes fixed)
- Bit-exact golden (magic 0x4D56_4357, version 1, csum_type=CRC32C).
- Magic/version/csum-type mismatch rejection.
- Short-payload rejection.

## Commit record (variable)
- Zero-writes minimum record (MIN_COMMIT_RECORD_SIZE = 29).
- Single-write with 64-byte payload + explicit byte-length arithmetic assertion.
- Multi-write with ordering preservation.
- Zero-length payload per write (kernel JBD2 revoke-block analogue).
- u64 bounds on CommitSeq/TxnId/BlockNumber.
- `commit_byte_size` contract across 0..5 writes; returns None for <4-byte input.

## Corruption detection
- CRC mismatch → Corrupted.
- Truncated mid-record → NeedMore(full_size).
- Zero record_len with non-zero tail → Corrupted.
- Empty input → EndOfData.
- All-zero tail → EndOfData.
- record_len below minimum → Corrupted.
- Unknown record type → Corrupted.
- num_writes past body capacity → early Corrupted (DoS guard).

Run: `rch exec -- env CARGO_TARGET_DIR=/tmp/rch_target_ffs_mvcc_cc_conformance cargo test -p ffs-mvcc --test conformance_wal_golden`.

Divergences at `crates/ffs-mvcc/tests/DISCREPANCIES.md` (3 ACCEPTED: CRC32C-only, COMMIT-only record type, all-zero-tail EndOfData sentinel).

Added `crc32c` to dev-dependencies so the corruption-detection tests can re-stamp CRCs after mutating the record body.","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-04-24T22:34:10.321008Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-04-24T22:34:10.321008Z","closed_at":"2026-04-24T22:34:10.321008Z","close_reason":"20 tests green via rch; fixtures inlined, DISCREPANCIES.md documented","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}
 {"id":"bd-z15u","title":"e2compr: implement single-indirect block pointer read-modify-write","description":"Implement write support for indirect block pointers (i_block[12] -> pointer block -> data). This is the first level of indirect addressing and covers logical blocks 12 through 12+ptrs_per_block-1 (for 4K blocks: 12 through 1035).

STEPS:
1. When logical_block >= 12 and < 12 + ptrs_per_block:
   a. Read i_block[12]. If 0, allocate a new block, zero-fill it, write i_block[12].
   b. Read the indirect block.
   c. Write the target pointer at the correct 4-byte offset within it.
   d. Write the indirect block back to disk.
2. For freeing: when all pointers in the indirect block become 0, free the indirect block itself and set i_block[12] = 0.
3. Update set_indirect_block_ptr to dispatch to this path when lb >= 12 && lb < 12 + ptrs_per_block.

ACCEPTANCE: Compressed write works for files up to 4MB (1024 clusters * 4 blocks * 4K = 16MB with shift=2).","status":"closed","priority":2,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:08:07.272650906Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:28.119620954Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:28.119131667Z","close_reason":"Added write_block_ptr() with single-indirect allocation + read-modify-write.
